title: SEGMENT 6 — The 2006 Cell paper: mouse iPSCs

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — The 2006 Cell paper: mouse iPSCs
The paper is titled "Induction of Pluripotent Stem Cells from Mouse Embryonic and Adult Fibroblast Cultures by Defined Factors.
" It's published in Cell in August 2006.
Two authors: Kazutoshi Takahashi and Shinya Yamanaka.
Takahashi was the postdoc who did most of the experimental work; Yamanaka was the senior author who designed the strategy.
The paper is twelve pages long, dense with data, and it is, without exaggeration, one of the most consequential biology papers of the twenty-first century.
Here's what they showed.
They took mouse fibroblasts — both embryonic fibroblasts and adult tail-tip fibroblasts — and they introduced the four factors using a retroviral vector.
After several weeks in ES cell culture conditions, small colonies appeared that looked like ES cells.
They were morphologically distinct from the original fibroblasts.
They had a high nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio.
They grew in tight, dome-shaped colonies.
They expressed the ES cell markers Yamanaka had been screening for.
Then they ran the full battery of pluripotency tests.
Test one: gene expression.
Did the reprogrammed cells turn on the genes that ES cells turn on, and turn off the genes fibroblasts had been expressing?
Answer: yes.
The transcriptomes — the full pattern of which genes are being read by the cell — shifted dramatically from a fibroblast pattern to an ES-cell-like pattern.
Test two: morphology and self-renewal.
Did the cells look like ES cells under the microscope, and did they grow indefinitely in culture without differentiating?
Answer: yes.
They could be passaged many times — meaning split into new dishes over and over again — without losing their identity.
Test three: differentiation potential in vitro.

If you put ES cells in a dish without the right maintenance factors, they spontaneously start differentiating into all three germ layers — ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm — forming structures called embryoid bodies.
Did the reprogrammed cells do this?
Answer: yes.
They formed embryoid bodies with cells expressing markers of all three germ layers.
Test four: teratoma formation.
This is the gold-standard test for pluripotency, and I'll spend some time on it later because it's both elegant and a little gruesome.
If you inject true pluripotent cells under the skin of an immunocompromised mouse, they form a benign tumor called a teratoma — a chaotic mass containing cells from all three germ layers.
Bone, gut lining, hair follicles, neurons, muscle, all jumbled together in a single tumor.
It looks like an embryo had a really bad day.
Did Yamanaka's reprogrammed cells form teratomas?
Answer: yes.
They produced classic three-germ-layer teratomas indistinguishable from those formed by ES cells.
By every test the field had developed, these reprogrammed cells were pluripotent.
Yamanaka named them induced pluripotent stem cells, or iPS cells, or iPSCs.

The name distinguishes them from ES cells — embryonic stem cells, which come from embryos — and emphasizes that pluripotency was induced, manufactured, in cells that had previously been something else.
The first round of iPSCs, frankly, were not quite as good as ES cells.
They were generated using a less stringent reporter — the Fbx15 system, which selects for cells that have started down the path of pluripotency but doesn't guarantee they've fully reached it.
The 2006 cells couldn't, when injected into early embryos, contribute to all tissues of the resulting mouse — a more stringent test of pluripotency called germline transmission.
So there was some immediate skepticism in the field.
Maybe these weren't really pluripotent.
Maybe they were just partially reprogrammed cells that had picked up some ES cell features without truly resetting all the way.

Yamanaka heard the criticism, went back to the bench, and a year later — in 2007 — published a follow-up paper using a more stringent reporter, this time the Nanog gene, which is on only in fully pluripotent cells.
Cells selected with the Nanog reporter were genuinely indistinguishable from ES cells.
They contributed to all tissues of chimeric mice.
They could even produce sperm and eggs and pass on the iPSC genome to offspring — the ultimate test, called germline transmission.
iPSCs were the real deal.
The community's response to the 2006 paper was, initially, somewhere between skepticism and disbelief.
The result was so unexpected, the methodology so seemingly simple, the implications so vast, that people assumed there must be something wrong with it.
Maybe Yamanaka was using contaminated ES cells.
Maybe the retroviruses were doing something unexpected.
Maybe the reprogramming was incomplete and Yamanaka was overclaiming.
Within months, those doubts were settled by an unusual mechanism: dozens of labs around the world tried to repeat the experiment.
The protocol was published in detail.
The four factors were known.
The cells were available.
And it worked.
In lab after lab, putting OSKM into fibroblasts and waiting several weeks produced colonies of cells that looked, behaved, and tested like ES cells.
The reproducibility of Yamanaka's result was, by 2007, undeniable.
He had not made it up.
The biology was real.
And the question on everyone's mind, immediately, was: does this work in human cells?
Because mouse biology and human biology are similar in many ways but they're not identical.
ES cells from the two species are subtly different.
Maybe human cells were locked down in ways mouse cells weren't.
Maybe OSKM wouldn't be enough.
Maybe you'd need different factors entirely.
The race to make human iPSCs was on, and it was going to be settled with surprising speed.
